news_summary: Artemis II captured stunning images of Earth illuminated by moonlight, showcasing remarkable progress in lunar exploration.
news_summary_fr: La mission Artemis II a capturé de superbes images de la Terre baignée par le clair de lune, illustrant les progrès remarquables réalisés dans l'exploration lunaire.
news_summary_es: La misión Artemis II capturó impresionantes imágenes de la Tierra iluminada por la luz de la luna, lo que pone de manifiesto los notables avances en la exploración lunar.

articles.title: A Moonlit Earth as Seen From Artemis II
articles.summary: An astronaut’s photo, taken en route to the Moon, reveals our planet and its place in space in a novel way.
articles.content:
April 2, 2026

One of the first images transmitted back to Earth from the Artemis II mission was a stunner. In a single image, Earth’s full disk appears amid celestial phenomena that illustrate its place in the solar system. And although the visible hemisphere appears to be awash in sunlight, it is actually lit by moonlight. The astronauts’ vantage point provided a rare opportunity to capture nighttime features—most notably lights from human habitation—from a new perspective.

An Artemis crew member captured the photo from the Orion spacecraft after it completed the translunar injection burn, which sent the spacecraft out of Earth orbit and on a trajectory toward the Moon. In the photo, Earth eclipses the Sun from Orion’s perspective, leaving only a small sliver of its bright light visible around the bottom right edge. Green auroras, caused by charged particles from the Sun interacting with Earth’s upper atmosphere, glow around the north and south poles (lower left and upper right, respectively).

The Sun’s light also produces the fuzzy glow, known as zodiacal light, that appears to the lower right of Earth. This phenomenon comes from sunlight reflecting off interplanetary dust. Skywatchers on Earth may see it at certain times of year around dawn or dusk as a faint column of light extending up from the horizon. Data collected by NASA’s Juno spacecraft on its journey to Jupiter suggest that Mars may be a significant source of the dust particles that produce zodiacal light. Earth’s other planetary neighbor, Venus, appears as the bright object in the bottom right of the image.

April 2, 2026

On Earth itself, city lights are evidence of human activity. Bright areas appear in Spain, Portugal, and northern Africa (lower left), sub-Saharan Africa (center left), and Brazil (center right). Digital camera technology—with help from the illumination of a full Moon—made it possible to see these and other details of Earth’s surface and atmosphere in low light. The crew set the camera’s ISO to 51,200 to make it highly sensitive to light. For comparison, an ISO setting of 100 or 200 is common for daytime photography.

Previous nighttime views of Earth taken from spacecraft may look very different from this photo but have also inspired and enlightened. For instance, the Apollo 12 crew photographed Earth eclipsing the Sun in 1969; astronaut Alan Bean would go on to depict his impressions of the event in paintings.

More recently, astronauts aboard the International Space Station have photographed the planet at night from low Earth orbit, while NASA’s Black Marble nighttime lights product suite uses satellite observations to produce science-quality records of nighttime lights at daily, monthly, and yearly time scales. Those programs provide sustained data records, while the Artemis II photo is distinctive as a single human-captured full-disk view showing many low-light features at once.

Cindy Evans, senior exploration scientist in the Astromaterials Research and Exploration Science Division at NASA’s Johnson Space Center, was working in the Science Evaluation Room during the Artemis II mission and was one of the first people on Earth to see the image. Evans was struck both by its beauty and the perspective revealed by all the visible solar system features. “I love the image so much because it was taken with Earth in moonshine, and shows Earth as a solar system body, a dynamic planet interacting with the solar wind, and a place harboring life,” she said.

The image is scientifically valuable, as well, said Miguel Román, Deputy Director for Atmospheres and Data Systems at NASA’s Goddard Space Flight Center. “It speaks powerfully to the breadth of what NASA does across science and human exploration,” he said. Román studies artificial light at night, as viewed from space, as a measurable signal of human activity.

“[This photo] reminds us that Earth at night is visually compelling, physically complex, and scientifically underexplored,” Román said. “I see this image as a glimpse of what Earth science can become in the future.”

NASA images prepared for Earth Observatory by Lauren Dauphin. Story by Lindsey Doermann.

References & Resources
articles.reasoning: The article describes a rare and scientifically valuable photograph of Earth taken from the Artemis II mission, providing a novel perspective on Earth's place in the solar system and human activity visible at night. The image has broad significance for science and human exploration, inspiring curiosity and appreciation for our planet and space exploration.
articles.personality_title: Artemis II captures rare moonlit photo of Earth from space
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – On April 2, 2026, NASA’s Artemis II mission traveled toward the Moon, offering astronauts a unique chance to photograph Earth from far away. This mission follows years of space exploration that have shown Earth from different views, but this image stands out for its clarity and details.

**What happened** – An astronaut aboard the Orion spacecraft took a striking photo showing the full Earth lit by moonlight, not sunlight. The picture was taken after the spacecraft left Earth’s orbit and headed to the Moon. It shows Earth partly blocking the Sun, glowing green auroras at the poles, and a faint glow called zodiacal light caused by sunlight reflecting off dust between planets. City lights from places like Spain, Africa, and Brazil are clearly visible, made possible by the camera’s high sensitivity to light.

**Impact** – This photo is special because it shows Earth as a bright and active planet in the solar system from a new angle. The image reveals natural light effects like auroras and zodiacal light, alongside human-made city lights at night. Scientists find it valuable for studying Earth’s atmosphere and human activity. It adds to what we know about Earth from space and shows the complex and beautiful nature of our planet.

**What's next step** – The Artemis II photo encourages more detailed Earth observations from space, especially at night. Scientists hope to use such images to better understand how Earth interacts with space weather and how human activity appears from orbit. Future missions may capture more images like this, helping researchers track environmental changes and improve space exploration.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A rare moonlit photo from Artemis II reveals Earth’s natural beauty and human activity from space, offering new insights into our planet and its place in the solar system.
articles.personality_title_fr: Artemis II capture une rare photo de la Terre éclairée par la Lune depuis l’espace
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le 2 avril 2026, la mission Artemis II de la NASA s’est dirigée vers la Lune, offrant aux astronautes une occasion unique de photographier la Terre vue de loin. Cette mission fait suite à des années d’exploration spatiale montrant la Terre sous différents angles, mais cette image se distingue par sa clarté et ses détails.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Un astronaute à bord du vaisseau Orion a pris une photo saisissante montrant la pleine Terre éclairée par la lumière de la Lune, et non celle du Soleil. La photo a été prise après que le vaisseau ait quitté l’orbite terrestre pour se diriger vers la Lune. Elle montre la Terre partiellement devant le Soleil, des aurores vertes aux pôles, et une faible lueur appelée lumière zodiacale, causée par la réflexion de la lumière solaire sur la poussière interplanétaire. Les lumières des villes comme en Espagne, en Afrique et au Brésil sont clairement visibles grâce à la grande sensibilité de la caméra.

**Impact** – Cette photo est unique car elle montre la Terre comme une planète active et lumineuse dans le système solaire sous un angle nouveau. L’image révèle des phénomènes naturels comme les aurores et la lumière zodiacale, ainsi que les lumières artificielles des villes la nuit. Les scientifiques la trouvent précieuse pour étudier l’atmosphère terrestre et l’activité humaine. Elle enrichit notre compréhension de la Terre depuis l’espace et montre la complexité et la beauté de notre planète.

**Prochaine étape** – La photo d’Artemis II encourage des observations plus détaillées de la Terre depuis l’espace, surtout la nuit. Les chercheurs espèrent utiliser ces images pour mieux comprendre l’interaction de la Terre avec le vent solaire et comment l’activité humaine se voit depuis l’orbite. Les futures missions pourraient capturer plus de photos similaires pour suivre les changements environnementaux et améliorer l’exploration spatiale.

**En une phrase** – Une rare photo de la Terre éclairée par la Lune prise par Artemis II révèle la beauté naturelle et l’activité humaine vue depuis l’espace, offrant de nouvelles connaissances sur notre planète et sa place dans le système solaire.
articles.personality_title_es: Artemis II captura una rara foto de la Tierra iluminada por la Luna desde el espacio
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El 2 de abril de 2026, la misión Artemis II de la NASA se dirigió a la Luna, brindando a los astronautas una oportunidad única para fotografiar la Tierra desde lejos. Esta misión sigue años de exploración espacial que han mostrado la Tierra desde diferentes perspectivas, pero esta imagen destaca por su claridad y detalles.

**Qué pasó** – Un astronauta a bordo de la nave Orion tomó una foto impresionante que muestra la Tierra completa iluminada por la luz de la Luna, no por la del Sol. La imagen fue tomada después de que la nave salió de la órbita terrestre y se dirigió hacia la Luna. Muestra la Tierra bloqueando parcialmente el Sol, auroras verdes en los polos y un brillo tenue llamado luz zodiacal, causado por la luz solar reflejada en el polvo entre los planetas. Las luces de ciudades en lugares como España, África y Brasil son claramente visibles gracias a la alta sensibilidad de la cámara.

**Impacto** – Esta foto es especial porque muestra la Tierra como un planeta activo y brillante en el sistema solar desde un ángulo nuevo. La imagen revela fenómenos naturales como las auroras y la luz zodiacal, junto con las luces artificiales de las ciudades por la noche. Los científicos la consideran valiosa para estudiar la atmósfera terrestre y la actividad humana. Aporta más conocimiento sobre la Tierra desde el espacio y muestra la complejidad y belleza de nuestro planeta.

**Próximo paso** – La foto de Artemis II impulsa observaciones más detalladas de la Tierra desde el espacio, especialmente de noche. Los científicos esperan usar imágenes así para entender mejor cómo la Tierra interactúa con el viento solar y cómo se ve la actividad humana desde la órbita. Futuras misiones podrían capturar más fotos similares para seguir cambios ambientales y avanzar en la exploración espacial.

**Una frase para recordar** – Una rara foto iluminada por la Luna tomada por Artemis II muestra la belleza natural y la actividad humana desde el espacio, ofreciendo nuevas ideas sobre nuestro planeta y su lugar en el sistema solar.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing full Earth seen from deep space, illuminated softly by moonlight with shimmering city lights visible on the continents; surrounded by ethereal green auroras at the poles and a faint, misty zodiacal light glow nearby, with a bright Venus shining gently in the background, all set against a serene cosmic backdrop that conveys Earth’s dynamic presence within the solar system.
